title: Self-healable chitosan-based polymer binder for anode in lithium-ion batteries
abstract_summary: This study presents a bio-based and self-healable polymeric binder designed for use in graphite-based anodes to achieve enhanced battery performance. The binder was synthesized via a Schiff base reaction between chitosan and a vanillin-derived linker, forming imine bonds that establish a self-healable 3D network. The bio-based binder effectively holds the graphite particles together while providing excellent mechanical strength, thermal stability, and strong adhesion to the copper foil. Notably, it demonstrated self-healing behavior, with mechanical recovery exceeding 90% after damage, and achieved a capacity of 161.2 mAh g⁻¹ with 81% capacity retention after 250 cycles.
green_strategy: Synthesis of a biodegradable, bio-based binder from chitosan (derived from seafood waste/chitin) and vanillin, eliminating the need for toxic fluorinated binders like PVDF and hazardous organic solvents like NMP.
fabrication_method: Schiff base reaction at room temperature between chitosan and a vanillin-derived aldehyde cross-linker to form dynamic covalent imine networks, followed by slurry casting.

performance_metrics.condition: CSF2 binder formulation on graphite anode
performance_metrics.comparison_baseline: 0.2 N for PVDF and 0.8 N for pristine chitosan

performance_metrics.condition: 0.5C rate cycling in graphite coin cells
performance_metrics.comparison_baseline: 73 % for PVDF binder and <70 % for pristine chitosan binder
performance_metrics.source_section_or_page: Page 7, Section 2.7 and Table 1 on Page 9

performance_metrics.condition: 0.5C rate during cycling of CSF2-based anode
performance_metrics.comparison_baseline: ca. 129 mAh g⁻¹ for PVDF-based anode

limitations: Dense polymeric network formed by imine bonds and the chitosan backbone restricts the free volume available for ion transport, resulting in lower ionic conductivity.
Trade-off between mechanical robustness, self-healing capability, and ionic conductivity of the chitosan-based binder.

figure_or_table_evidence.evidence_summary: SEM cross-sectional images showing the thickness change of graphite electrodes after 20 cycles. The CSF2-based electrode expanded from 45 µm to 61.89 µm, while the PVDF-based electrode expanded to 80.20 µm, demonstrating that the CSF2 binder suppresses electrode swelling.

Comprehensive comparison of electrochemical properties of CS, PVDF, and CSF2 binders, explicitly detailing initial capacity, capacity retention, cycling stability, Coulombic efficiency, rate capability, charge-discharge profiles, impedance, volume change, and self-healing ability.

candidate_research_claims.claim_text: Both coin and pouch cells fabricated with the bio-based binder achieved a specific capacity of 161.2 mAh g-1 and retained 81 % of their capacity after 250 cycles.
candidate_research_claims.limitation: The dense network slightly limits the free volume for Li-ion transport.
candidate_research_claims.citation_text: Both coin and pouch cells fabricated with the bio-based binder achieved a specific capacity of 161.2 mAh g-1 and retained 81 % of their capacity after 250 cycles.
